hero_image.alt: Portrait of W. E. B. Du Bois, 1907
hero_image.caption: W. E. B. Du Bois in 1907, four years after The Souls of Black Folk appeared.
hero_image.license: Public domain; NPG digital image released CC0

inline_images.alt: Four African American women seated on the steps of a building at Atlanta University, Georgia, 1899 or 1900
inline_images.caption: Four young women on the steps at Atlanta University around 1900, from the photographs Du Bois assembled for the Paris Exposition, the institution whose students he casts as the wings bearing Black youth toward knowledge.
inline_images.repository: Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (African American Photographs Assembled for the 1900 Paris Exposition)
inline_images.license: Public domain (no known restrictions on publication)

related_chapters.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
related_chapters.part_title: A Family of Twelve in Warren County
related_chapters.chapter_title: This One Will Go to College
related_chapters.topic: A Mother's Hope and a We
related_chapters.why: Du Bois frames the Southern university as the goal that lifts a people beyond material striving, and this chapter is a mother singling out one of twelve children for college, the aspiration that carried Terry to Bennett.

related_chapters.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
related_chapters.why: The essay treats the college as the place that forms whole people rather than bread-winners, and Sellers reflects on the movement itself as a university, an education in purpose rather than pay.

related_chapters.subject: Lonnie C. King
related_chapters.why: Du Bois names Atlanta's college youth as the rising generation, and King recounts the Spelman students of the Atlanta University Center who carried the 1960 student movement.

related_chapters.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
related_chapters.chapter_title: It Does Not Have to Be This Way
related_chapters.why: The essay argues higher learning, not money-getting, is the end of education, and Hrabowski describes the Meyerhoff program he built to produce Black PhDs in science at scale.

related_chapters.subject: John Churchville
related_chapters.topic: Why a Library Became a School
related_chapters.why: Du Bois roots the common school on the university and the search for knowledge, and Churchville explains why his North Philadelphia Freedom Library grew into a school.

related_chapters.subject: Julia Matilda Burns
related_chapters.chapter_title: She Saw What I Could Not See in Myself
related_chapters.why: The essay casts the teacher as the bearer of culture to an untaught people, and Burns credits a tenth-grade English teacher who recognized a capacity she had not seen in herself.

top_voice.subject: Freeman A. Hrabowski III
top_voice.reason: Du Bois calls the universities the wings that bear youth past the temptation of mere gold toward knowledge, and Hrabowski's Meyerhoff Scholars Program producing Black scientists is that argument enacted a century later.

closing_image.alt: Freeman A. Hrabowski III at the 2012 Time 100 gala
closing_image.caption: Freeman A. Hrabowski III, the corpus voice this essay connects to most. Du Bois calls the universities the wings that bear youth past the temptation of mere gold toward knowledge, and Hrabowski's Meyerhoff Scholars Program producing Black scientists is that argument enacted a century later.

connections.summary: Across the Civil Rights History Project, the oral histories below take up the questions Of the Wings of Atalanta raises. Our embedding model places it nearest the testimony of Annie Pearl Avery, Sam Young, Jr., and Amos C. Brown.

nearest_voices.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
